network.overall_strategy: This network reflects the literature consensus that prebiotic arginine synthesis is unresolved and most plausibly proceeds by a two-phase strategy: (1) generation of a C5 amino backbone or an arginine aminonitrile precursor through hydrothermal carbon fixation and/or surface cyanosulfidic chemistry, and (2) installation of the guanidinium group by cyanamide or cyanate chemistry, followed by hydrolysis and biochemical concentration/assembly. The network therefore centers on four hub intermediates—cyanamide, ornithine, beta-aminopropionaldehyde, and the arginine aminonitrile precursor—and connects hydrothermal production of simple carbon/nitrogen feedstocks with surface photochemistry and biochemical concentration on clays. Ten distinct pathways are included, but several remain explicitly speculative where the literature identifies open gaps, especially conversion from simple feedstocks to ornithine and continuous demonstration to arginine.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed reduction of carbon dioxide to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent setting, anoxic, H2-rich, pH 9-11

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface electron transfer reduces CO2 with H2 to one-carbon reduced products on Fe-S phases.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate on iron-sulfur minerals is central to vent chemistry and consistent with Fe-S protometabolism models and greigite's ferredoxin-like role (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997; Jordan et al., 2021). Thermodynamic favorability near alkaline vent mixing interfaces was analyzed by Kitadai (2015).

reactions.name: Magnetite/greigite-mediated pyruvate synthesis from reduced carbon

conditions.other: subseafloor/alkaline vent, anoxic, reductive carbon fixation regime

reactions.mechanism: Sequential mineral-catalyzed C1/C2 carbon fixation and reduction generate an alpha-keto acid framework culminating in pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: The network requires hydrothermal alpha-keto acid generation because pyruvate is a plausible central metabolic hub. This step follows the user-specified hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis motif and is framed by vent carbon-fixation chemistry, though direct prebiotic pyruvate synthesis remains less experimentally resolved than simpler C1/C2 products; included as a necessary but partly speculative connector consistent with hydrothermal protometabolism (Kitadai, 2015; Amend & Shock, 1998; Jordan et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Supercritical CO2/H2O trace arginine formation from pyruvate and hydroxylamine

conditions.catalyst: pyrite and elemental iron as plausible hydrothermal mineral context
conditions.other: supercritical CO2/H2O; product detected after acid hydrolysis; trace yield

reactions.mechanism: Poorly resolved multistep hydrothermal reaction network from pyruvic acid and hydroxylamine gives trace arginine after hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Fujioka et al. (2009) reported 0.32 umol arginine from hydroxylamine hydrochloride and pyruvic acid under supercritical CO2/H2O at 60C and >73 atm, but did not elucidate mechanism. This is the only direct abiotic arginine claim in the literature and is therefore included despite mechanistic uncertainty. The mineral agents are assigned to satisfy hydrothermal-network constraints and reflect Fe-mineral-rich hydrothermal settings; this catalytic role is speculative and should be treated as such (Fujioka et al., 2009; Cleaves et al., 2010).

reactions.name: UV photochemical formation of cyanamide from reduced atmospheric feedstocks

conditions.other: UV irradiation of reduced atmospheric CH4/NH3/H2O-derived surface condensates under anoxic conditions

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven atmospheric/surface photochemistry forms cyanamide, which accumulates in evaporitic or shallow aqueous environments.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanamide is a key guanylating agent and has strong prebiotic standing from UV irradiation of reduced gas mixtures; Schimpl, Lemmon & Calvin (1965) demonstrated photochemical formation. Patel et al. (2015) further place cyanamide as a central node in cyanosulfidic protometabolism. TiO2 is used here as the required surface photocatalyst consistent with mineral-assisted UV chemistry.

reactions.name: HCN oligomer photochemistry to adenine
reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes through diaminomaleonitrile-like intermediates to purine nucleobases under irradiation and concentration cycles.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization is a canonical surface prebiotic route to nucleobases and situates the arginine network within broader cyanosulfidic protometabolism, where shared feedstocks produce amino acid and nucleotide precursors simultaneously (Ferris et al., 1978; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: UV ammonium cyanide photolysis to guanidine

conditions.catalyst: iron-assisted UV photolysis on mineral surface
conditions.other: 254 nm UV, pH 8.4-8.5, 7 days to 10 weeks, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Photolysis of ammonium cyanide solutions yields guanidine through cyanide-derived oligomerization and hydrolysis pathways.
reactions.reasoning: Lohrmann (1972) demonstrated guanidine formation in 8-20% yield from UV irradiation of ammonium cyanide with iron catalyst at pH 8.4-8.5. Guanidine is thus a robust nitrogen-rich side product in HCN chemistry, though not by itself a demonstrated route to arginine. Mineralized iron/TiO2 surfaces are used to satisfy surface-agent requirements while remaining close to the reported iron-catalyzed photochemistry (Lohrmann, 1972; Robertson & Miller, 1995).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic cross-coupling of HCN and acetylene to acrylonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system associated with sulfide mineral setting
conditions.other: 35C, UV light, anaerobic, phosphate-buffered cyanosulfidic chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Cu-mediated photoredox cross-coupling joins cyanide-derived carbon with acetylene to give acrylonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) reported the cyanosulfidic protometabolism network at 35C under anaerobic UV conditions with Cu(I)/Cu(II), H2S, and phosphate, including formation of acrylonitrile from HCN and acetylene; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017) confirmed the route. Sulfide minerals and phosphate minerals are included because the chemistry is explicitly cyanosulfidic and phosphate-buffered (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Addition of ammonia to acrylonitrile to form beta-aminopropionitrile
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic conjugate addition of ammonia to activated nitrile alkene furnishes beta-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This step is explicitly part of the Patel et al. (2015) arginine/proline precursor branch and was discussed further by Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017). Mineral surfaces are assigned to match the sulfur-rich cyanosulfidic setting and clay concentration in surface pools.

reactions.name: Photoreduction of beta-aminopropionitrile to beta-aminopropionaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction of the nitrile branch yields the corresponding amino aldehyde, a key guanylation substrate.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) and Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017) place beta-aminopropionaldehyde in the cyanosulfidic route to arginine precursor 46. This aldehyde is the key branchpoint before guanylation.

reactions.name: Efficient guanylation of beta-aminopropionaldehyde by cyanamide

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration surfaces in cyanosulfidic pools
conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, UV-driven network context; especially efficient guanylation

reactions.mechanism: Primary amine addition to cyanamide installs a guanidino functionality on the amino aldehyde branch, leading onward to the arginine aminonitrile precursor.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) explicitly describe the especially efficient reaction of beta-aminopropionaldehyde with cyanamide as the entry to arginine precursor chemistry, and Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017) confirmed the pathway. Montmorillonite is added as a plausible concentrating mineral compatible with amine-rich surface pools.

reactions.name: Further photoreduction and cyanide addition to generate arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor
reactions.mechanism: Within the cyanosulfidic network, photoreduction and HCN addition transform the guanylated intermediate into compound 46, the arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) identify compound 46 as the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine, formed downstream of beta-aminopropionaldehyde guanylation; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017) confirmed the route. The precursor and the product are represented by the same molecule node because the literature report emphasizes compound 46 as the operative hub rather than all upstream unresolved micro-intermediates.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor to arginine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis in prebiotic pools; prolonged timescale

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis converts the alpha-aminonitrile precursor into the corresponding alpha-amino acid arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) and Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017) indicate that hydrolysis of compound 46 would furnish arginine. The full continuous experiment to arginine has not been demonstrated, so this step is inferred from standard aminonitrile-to-amino-acid chemistry and remains a literature-grounded but incompletely validated completion step.

reactions.name: Direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide to arginine

conditions.other: aqueous/basic guanylation; classical Ba(OH)2-assisted chemistry and modern high-yield aqueous catalysis known, but not demonstrated under fully prebiotic conditions

reactions.mechanism: The delta-amino group of ornithine attacks cyanamide, installing the guanidinium group directly to yield arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the best-defined conceptual route to arginine: first demonstrated by Schulze & Winterstein (1899) and later shown to be highly effective in water by Tsubokura et al. (2014), though the latter uses a non-prebiotic catalyst. The reaction is therefore chemically validated but prebiotically unresolved. The absence of a mineral agent reflects that this is placed in the biochemical/prebiotic stage rather than a strict surface mineral-catalyzed step (Schulze & Winterstein, 1899; Tsubokura et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Carbamoylation of ornithine by cyanate to citrulline

conditions.other: anoxic aqueous pool; cyanate used as stable carbamoyl equivalent

reactions.mechanism: The terminal amino group of ornithine is carbamoylated by cyanate to form citrulline, a urea-cycle analog intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Juarez et al. (2021) argued cyanate is a kinetically plausible prebiotic carbamoyl donor compared with carbamoyl phosphate, and Ferris et al. (1978) identified citrulline in HCN oligomer hydrolysates. This route is hypothetical but directly motivated by the literature's proposed prebiotic urea-cycle analog.

reactions.name: Conversion of citrulline to arginine in a prebiotic urea-cycle analog

conditions.other: concentrated prebiotic pool; entirely hypothetical nonenzymatic analog

reactions.mechanism: Further guanidino-group elaboration from citrulline-like intermediates yields arginine in a nonenzymatic urea-cycle analog.
reactions.reasoning: The literature presents a prebiotic urea-cycle analog as a hypothesis rather than an experimentally demonstrated route. This step is explicitly speculative but necessary to complete the network from the cited citrulline precedent to arginine, and is consistent with the review's statement that all key intermediates have prebiotic precedent while the full route remains undemonstrated (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021; Cunin et al., 1986).

reactions.name: Prebiotic production of ornithine from glutamic acid-rich mixtures

conditions.other: concentrated prebiotic pool; hypothetical backbone elaboration step

reactions.mechanism: Nonenzymatic backbone and amination chemistry converts glutamate-related precursors to ornithine.
reactions.reasoning: Ornithine is experimentally detected in Miller volcanic spark discharge products and tentatively in meteorites, while glutamic acid is abundant across prebiotic sources. However, no definitive prebiotic conversion from glutamate to ornithine is established, so this node-connecting step is explicitly speculative and included only because the literature identifies glutamate as the biosynthetic starting point and ornithine as the immediate precursor required for direct guanylation (Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011; Meierhenrich et al., 2004; Naraoka et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Montmorillonite adsorption and concentration of arginine

conditions.other: pH 5-7 clay interlayers; selective cation exchange and UV/thermal protection

reactions.mechanism: Arginine partitions into montmorillonite interlayers through cation exchange of its guanidinium group, increasing local concentration and persistence.
reactions.reasoning: Catalano et al. (2024) showed montmorillonite selectively adsorbs arginine and lysine 30-50x more strongly than neutral amino acids, providing a plausible post-synthesis concentration and protection mechanism. Zhou et al. (2019) further support reactivity of montmorillonite-arginine complexes under dry heating. This is not a synthetic step but is essential for network realism once arginine is formed.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic route to arginine
pathways.description: The most literature-supported route: HCN and acetylene enter Sutherland's cyanosulfidic network, producing beta-aminopropionaldehyde, which reacts efficiently with cyanamide to give compound 46, then hydrolyzes to arginine.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine-cyanamide guanylation
pathways.description: The simplest conceptual route: prebiotically available ornithine and cyanamide meet in a concentrated pool and react directly to form arginine.

pathways.name: Urea-cycle analog via citrulline
pathways.description: A hypothetical nonenzymatic route in which ornithine is carbamoylated by cyanate to citrulline and then converted onward to arginine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal carbon fixation feeding cyanosulfidic arginine synthesis
pathways.description: Hydrothermal fixation produces reduced carbon hubs that feed surface chemistry, ultimately joining the canonical cyanosulfidic branch to arginine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal trace direct arginine route
pathways.description: The only reported direct abiotic arginine synthesis claim from pyruvate and hydroxylamine under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions.

pathways.name: Cyanamide-first route through amino aldehyde guanylation
pathways.description: Surface photochemistry first accumulates cyanamide, which then guanylates beta-aminopropionaldehyde formed in the cyanosulfidic branch.

pathways.name: HCN-rich pool route coupling oligomer chemistry to arginine precursor formation
pathways.description: A shared HCN reservoir simultaneously supports nucleobase chemistry and the cyanosulfidic branch to arginine, reflecting convergent protometabolism.

pathways.name: Glu-to-Orn backbone assembly then guanylation
pathways.description: Glutamate-rich prebiotic mixtures generate ornithine, which then reacts with cyanamide to give arginine.

pathways.name: Glu-to-Orn then citrulline branch to arginine
pathways.description: Glutamate-derived ornithine enters the hypothetical cyanate/citrulline branch before conversion to arginine.

pathways.name: Arginine formation followed by clay concentration and persistence
pathways.description: Any route that reaches arginine is completed by selective montmorillonite adsorption, increasing persistence and local concentration for downstream chemistry.
